document_metadata.title: Shropshire nurse allowed to continue working after text about colleague
document_metadata.first_words: # Nurse can continue to work in current role - hearing - Published A nurse who said she had to stop herself from "gouging a former colleague's eyeballs out and shoving them down her throat" has been allowed to continue working in her current role. Deborah Povall was the manager of Powys ward at the Robert Jones and Agnes Hunt (RJAH) Orthopaedic Hospital in Gobowen, Shropshire, at the time she sent the text message to another colleague in 2018. She is currently facing a Nursing and Midwifery Council

chunks.content: A nurse who said she had to stop herself from “gouging a former colleague's eyeballs out and shoving them down her throat“ has been allowed to continue working in her current role. Deborah Povall was the manager of Powys ward at the Robert Jones and Agnes Hunt (RJAH) Orthopaedic Hospital in Gobowen, Shropshire, at the time she sent the text message to another colleague in 2018. She is currently facing a Nursing and Midwifery Council (NMC) hearing into allegations over how she treated staff and the care received by patients at the hospital. The NMC has now closed its case, with Ms Povall due to give evidence when it resumes in December. Ms Povall, who was suspended after an investigation was launched in 2019, has admitted working excessive hours but denied all of the other allegations relating to incidents between 2017 and 2019.
chunks.content_original: A nurse who said she had to stop herself from “gouging a former colleague's eyeballs out and shoving them down her throat“ has been allowed to continue working in her current role. Deborah Povall was the manager of Powys ward at the Robert Jones and Agnes Hunt (RJAH) Orthopaedic Hospital in Gobowen, Shropshire, at the time she sent the text message to another colleague in 2018. She is currently facing a Nursing and Midwifery Council (NMC) hearing into allegations over how she treated staff and the care received by patients at the hospital. The NMC has now closed its case, with Ms Povall due to give evidence when it resumes in December. Ms Povall, who was suspended after an investigation was launched in 2019, has admitted working excessive hours but denied all of the other allegations relating to incidents between 2017 and 2019.

chunks.content: The hearing heard on Thursday that since leaving the hospital, Ms Povall has been working for a company that provides healthcare assessments for the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP). The panel decided not to impose an interim order, meaning she can continue working in her current role. Ms Povall told the hearing she only works her current hours in her role and does not use social media or text. “My current role requires me to be a registered nurse; therefore, any interim order potentially may mean that I cannot do that depending on what that was,“ she explained. “My role involves me talking to individuals that are not acutely unwell. However, they have healthcare conditions, and my job is to ascertain them and the impact on their day-to-day function.“ Alastair Kennedy, case presenter for the NMC, confirmed Ms Povall has been free to work since the investigation began.
chunks.content_original: The hearing heard on Thursday that since leaving the hospital, Ms Povall has been working for a company that provides healthcare assessments for the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP). The panel decided not to impose an interim order, meaning she can continue working in her current role. Ms Povall told the hearing she only works her current hours in her role and does not use social media or text. “My current role requires me to be a registered nurse; therefore, any interim order potentially may mean that I cannot do that depending on what that was,“ she explained. “My role involves me talking to individuals that are not acutely unwell. However, they have healthcare conditions, and my job is to ascertain them and the impact on their day-to-day function.“ Alastair Kennedy, case presenter for the NMC, confirmed Ms Povall has been free to work since the investigation began.

chunks.content: “Two charges have been proved by way of admission: working excessive hours and sending an unfortunate text message,“ he said. “Neither of these issues raises public protection concerns to the extent that an interim order will be required. There is unlikely to be a repetition of working excessive hours or the text issue.“ Witness Kirsty Foskett said she had a good working relationship with Ms Povall but did speak to her about working too many hours, as she “felt a need to cover vacant shifts“. Mrs Foskett, who was the NMC's final witness, was the surgical matron of the five wards at the hospital at the time. The hearing heard Ms Povall would have the first level of approval for rotas, followed by herself. “I had been a previous ward manager, and you are responsible for the quality and safety on your ward,“ Mrs Foskett said. She told the hearing that a member of staff who was leaving raised concerns about Ms Povall's behaviour in November 2017, but she was “quite open“ and “reflective“ to the feedback. The hearing is due to continue on 15 December.
chunks.content_original: “Two charges have been proved by way of admission: working excessive hours and sending an unfortunate text message,“ he said. “Neither of these issues raises public protection concerns to the extent that an interim order will be required. There is unlikely to be a repetition of working excessive hours or the text issue.“ Witness Kirsty Foskett said she had a good working relationship with Ms Povall but did speak to her about working too many hours, as she “felt a need to cover vacant shifts“. Mrs Foskett, who was the NMC's final witness, was the surgical matron of the five wards at the hospital at the time. The hearing heard Ms Povall would have the first level of approval for rotas, followed by herself. “I had been a previous ward manager, and you are responsible for the quality and safety on your ward,“ Mrs Foskett said. She told the hearing that a member of staff who was leaving raised concerns about Ms Povall's behaviour in November 2017, but she was “quite open“ and “reflective“ to the feedback. The hearing is due to continue on 15 December.

e1y4nx260"><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">Deborah Povall was the manager at Powys Ward at the Robert Jones and Agnes Hunt Orthopaedic Hospital</p></div></figcaption></figure></div><div data-component="byline-block" class="ssrcss-1km5m62-BylineComponentWrapper e8mq1e90"><div data-testid="single-byline" class="ssrcss-sevsa1-ContributorContainer ee66fvo3"><div class="ssrcss-dt4z-ContributorInfo ee66fvo0"><span class="visually-hidden ssrcss-1f39n02-VisuallyHidden e16en2lz0">By</span><span class="ssrcss-vd0pba-TextContributorName epw3ir01">Paul Rogers</span><div class="ssrcss-dqo7s5-ContributorDetails ee66fvo2"><span class="ssrcss-c4qnun-Text ee66fvo4">Local Democracy Reporter</span></div></div></div></div><div data-component="metadata-block" class="ssrcss-1n2i08q-ComponentWrapper ep2nwvo2"><div><ul role="list" class="ssrcss-1q1gjvm-MetadataStripContainer eh44mf03"><div class="ssrcss-13nu8ri-GroupChildrenForWrapping eh44mf02"><li role="listitem" class="ssrcss-1dqo2s-MetadataStripItem eh44mf01"><div class="visually-hidden ssrcss-1f39n02-VisuallyHidden e16en2lz0">Published</div><div class="ssrcss-m5j4pi-MetadataContent eh44mf00"><span class="ssrcss-1pvwv4b-MetadataSnippet e4wm5bw3"><span class="ssrcss-61mhsj-MetadataText e4wm5bw1"><time data-testid="timestamp" dateTime="2025-08-29T14:56:09.365Z">29 August 2025</time></span></span></div></li></div></ul></div></div><div data-component="text-block" class="ssrcss-suhx0k-RichTextComponentWrapper ep2nwvo0"><div class="ssrcss-1o5p6v2-RichTextContainer e5tfeyi1"><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10"><b class="ssrcss-1xjjfut-BoldText e5tfeyi3">A nurse who said she had to stop herself from "gouging a former colleague's eyeballs out and shoving them down her throat" has been allowed to continue working in her current role.</b></p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">Deborah Povall was the manager of Powys ward at the Robert Jones and Agnes Hunt (RJAH) Orthopaedic Hospital in Gobowen, Shropshire, at the time she sent the text message to another colleague in 2018.</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">She is currently facing a Nursing and Midwifery Council (NMC) hearing into allegations over how she treated staff and the care received by patients at the hospital.</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">The NMC has now closed its case, with Ms Povall due to give evidence when it resumes in December.</p></div></div><div data-component="text-block" class="ssrcss-suhx0k-RichTextComponentWrapper ep2nwvo0"><div class="ssrcss-1o5p6v2-RichTextContainer e5tfeyi1"><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">Ms Povall, who was suspended after an investigation was launched in 2019, has admitted working excessive hours but denied all of the other allegations relating to incidents between 2017 and 2019.</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">The hearing heard on Thursday that since leaving the hospital, Ms Povall has been working for a company that provides healthcare assessments for the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP).</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">The panel decided not to impose an interim order, meaning she can continue working in her current role.</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">Ms Povall told the hearing she only works her current hours in her role and does not use social media or text.</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">"My current role requires me to be a registered nurse; therefore, any interim order potentially may mean that I cannot do that depending on what that was," she explained.</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">"My role involves me talking to individuals that are not acutely unwell. However, they have healthcare conditions, and my job is to ascertain them and the impact on their day-to-day function."</p></div></div><div data-component="text-block" class="ssrcss-suhx0k-RichTextComponentWrapper ep2nwvo0"><div class="ssrcss-1o5p6v2-RichTextContainer e5tfeyi1"><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">Alastair Kennedy, case presenter for the NMC, confirmed Ms Povall has been free to work since the investigation began.</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">"Two charges have been proved by way of admission: working excessive hours and sending an unfortunate text message," he said.</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">"Neither of these issues raises public protection concerns to the extent that an interim order will be required. There is unlikely to be a repetition of working excessive hours or the text issue."</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">Witness Kirsty Foskett said she had a good working relationship with Ms Povall but did speak to her about working too many hours, as she "felt a need to cover vacant shifts".</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">Mrs Foskett, who was the NMC's final witness, was the surgical matron of the five wards at the hospital at the time.</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">The hearing heard Ms Povall would have the first level of approval for rotas, followed by herself.</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">"I had been a previous ward manager, and you are responsible for the quality and safety on your ward," Mrs Foskett said.</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">She told the hearing that a member of staff who was leaving raised concerns about Ms Povall's behaviour in November 2017, but she was "quite open" and "reflective" to the feedback.</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">The hearing is due to continue on 15 December.</p></div></div><div data-component="text-block" class="ssrcss-suhx0k-RichTextComponentWrapper ep2nwvo0"><div class="ssrcss-1o5p6v2-RichTextContainer e5tfeyi1"><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10"><i class="ssrcss-xbdn93-ItalicText e5tfeyi2">This news was gathered by the </i><a href="/lnp/ldrs" class="ssrcss-f6h2dj-InlineLink e1kn3p7n0"><i class="ssrcss-xbdn93-ItalicText e5tfeyi2">Local Democracy Reporting Service</i></a><i class="ssrcss-xbdn93-ItalicText e5tfeyi2">, which covers councils and other public service organisations.</i></p></div></div><div data-component="embed-ugc-uploader-block" id="uploader-8-u184429132" class="ssrcss-bq6git-ComponentWrapper ep2nwvo2"><div class="ssrcss-dp75qe-UgcUploaderContent e1fzw31j1"><div class="ssrcss-18kcfkv-MessageWrapper elwmwb23"><div><h2 class="ssrcss-1uy3aq3-Title elwmwb22">Get in touch</h2><div class="ssrcss-jqctwy-TextMessageContainer elwmwb20"><div class="ssrcss-1o5p6v2-RichTextContainer e5tfeyi1"><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">Tell us which from \\\"gouging a former colleague's eyeballs out and shoving them down her throat\\\" has been allowed to continue working in her current role.\",\"blocks\":[{\"type\":\"fragment\",\"model\":{\"text\":\"A nurse who said she had to stop herself from \\\"gouging a former colleague's eyeballs out and shoving them down her throat\\\" has been allowed to continue working in her current role.\",\"attributes\":[\"bold\"]}}]}},{\"type\":\"paragraph\",\"model\":{\"text\":\"Deborah Povall was the manager of Powys ward at the Robert Jones and Agnes Hunt (RJAH) Orthopaedic Hospital in Gobowen, Shropshire, at the time she sent the text message to another colleague in 2018.\",\"blocks\":[{\"type\":\"fragment\",\"model\":{\"text\":\"Deborah Povall was the manager of Powys ward at the Robert Jones and Agnes Hunt (RJAH) Orthopaedic Hospital in Gobowen, Shropshire, at the time she sent the text message to another colleague in 2018.\",\"attributes\":[]}}]}},{\"type\":\"paragraph\",\"model\":{\"text\":\"She is currently facing a Nursing and Midwifery Council (NMC) hearing into allegations over how she treated staff and the care received by patients at the hospital.\",\"blocks\":[{\"type\":\"fragment\",\"model\":{\"text\":\"She is currently facing a Nursing and Midwifery Council (NMC) hearing into allegations over how she treated staff and the care received by patients at the hospital.\",\"attributes\":[]}}]}},{\"type\":\"paragraph\",\"model\":{\"text\":\"The NMC has now closed its case, with Ms Povall due to give evidence when it resumes in December.\",\"blocks\":[{\"type\":\"fragment\",\"model\":{\"text\":\"The NMC has now closed its case, with Ms Povall due to give evidence when it resumes in December.\",\"attributes\":[]}}]}}]}},{\"type\":\"text\",\"model\":{\"suitableForAbridgement\":false,\"blocks\":[{\"type\":\"paragraph\",\"model\":{\"text\":\"Ms Povall, who was suspended after an investigation was launched in 2019, has admitted working excessive hours but denied all of the other allegations relating to incidents between 2017 and 2019.\",\"blocks\":[{\"type\":\"fragment\",\"model\":{\"text\":\"Ms Povall, who was suspended after an investigation was launched in 2019, has admitted working excessive hours but denied all of the other allegations relating to incidents between 2017 and 2019.\",\"attributes\":[]}}]}},{\"type\":\"paragraph\",\"model\":{\"text\":\"The hearing heard on Thursday that since leaving the hospital, Ms Povall has been working for a company that provides healthcare assessments for the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP).\",\"blocks\":[{\"type\":\"fragment\",\"model\":{\"text\":\"The hearing heard on Thursday that since leaving the hospital, Ms Povall has been working for a company that provides healthcare assessments for the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP).\",\"attributes\":[]}}]}},{\"type\":\"paragraph\",\"model\":{\"text\":\"The panel decided not to impose an interim order, meaning she can continue working in her current role.\",\"blocks\":[{\"type\":\"fragment\",\"model\":{\"text\":\"The panel decided not to impose an interim order, meaning she can continue working in her current role.\",\"attributes\":[]}}]}},{\"type\":\"paragraph\",\"model\":{\"text\":\"Ms Povall told the hearing she only works her current hours in her role and does not use social media or text.\",\"blocks\":[{\"type\":\"fragment\",\"model\":{\"text\":\"Ms Povall told the hearing she only works her current hours in her role and does not use social media or text.\",\"attributes\":[]}}]}},{\"type\":\"paragraph\",\"model\":{\"text\":\"\\\"My current role requires me to be a registered nurse; therefore, any interim order potentially may mean that I cannot do that depending on what that was,\\\" she explained.\",\"blocks\":[{\"type\":\"fragment\",\"model\":{\"text\":\"\\\"My current role requires me to be a registered nurse; therefore, any interim order potentially may mean that I cannot do that depending on what that was,\\\" she explained.\",\"attributes\":[]}}]}},{\"type\":\"paragraph\",\"model\":{\"text\":\"\\\"My role involves me talking to individuals that are not acutely unwell. However, they have healthcare conditions, and my job is to ascertain them and the impact on their day-to-day function.\\\"\",\"blocks\":[{\"type\":\"fragment\",\"model\":{\"text\":\"\\\"My role involves me talking to individuals that are not acutely unwell. However, they have healthcare conditions, and my job is to ascertain them and the impact on their day-to-day function.\\\"\",\"attributes\":[]}}]}}]}},{\"type\":\"text\",\"model\":{\"suitableForAbridgement\":false,\"blocks\":[{\"type\":\"paragraph\",\"model\":{\"text\":\"Alastair Kennedy, case presenter for the NMC, confirmed Ms Povall has been free to work since the investigation began.\",\"blocks\":[{\"type\":\"fragment\",\"model\":{\"text\":\"Alastair Kennedy, case presenter for the NMC, confirmed Ms Povall has been free to work since the investigation began.\",\"attributes\":[]}}]}},{\"type\":\"paragraph\",\"model\":{\"text\":\"\\\"Two charges have been proved by way of admission: working excessive hours and sending an unfortunate text message,\\\" he said.\",\"blocks\":[{\"type\":\"fragment\",\"model\":{\"text\":\"\\\"Two charges have been proved by way of admission: working excessive hours and sending an unfortunate text message,\\\" he said.\",\"attributes\":[]}}]}},{\"type\":\"paragraph\",\"model\":{\"text\":\"\\\"Neither of these issues raises public protection concerns to the extent that an interim order will be required. There is unlikely to be a repetition of working excessive hours or the text issue.\\\"\",\"blocks\":[{\"type\":\"fragment\",\"model\":{\"text\":\"\\\"Neither of these issues raises public protection concerns to the extent that an interim order will be required. There is unlikely to be a repetition of working excessive hours or the text issue.\\\"\",\"attributes\":[]}}]}},{\"type\":\"paragraph\",\"model\":{\"text\":\"Witness Kirsty Foskett said she had a good working relationship with Ms Povall but did speak to her about working too many hours, as she \\\"felt a need to cover vacant shifts\\\".\",\"blocks\":[{\"type\":\"fragment\",\"model\":{\"text\":\"Witness Kirsty Foskett said she had a good working relationship with Ms Povall but did speak to her about working too many hours, as she \\\"felt a need to cover vacant shifts\\\".\",\"attributes\":[]}}]}},{\"type\":\"paragraph\",\"model\":{\"text\":\"Mrs Foskett, who was the NMC's final witness, was the surgical matron of the five wards at the hospital at the time.\",\"blocks\":[{\"type\":\"fragment\",\"model\":{\"text\":\"Mrs Foskett, who was the NMC's final witness, was the surgical matron of the five wards at the hospital at the time.\",\"attributes\":[]}}]}},{\"type\":\"paragraph\",\"model\":{\"text\":\"The hearing heard Ms Povall would have the first level of approval for rotas, followed by herself.\",\"blocks\":[{\"type\":\"fragment\",\"model\":{\"text\":\"The hearing heard Ms Povall would have the first level of approval for rotas, followed by herself.\",\"attributes\":[]}}]}},{\"type\":\"paragraph\",\"model\":{\"text\":\"\\\"I had been a previous ward manager, and you are responsible for the quality and safety on your ward,\\\" Mrs Foskett said.\",\"blocks\":[{\"type\":\"fragment\",\"model\":{\"text\":\"\\\"I had been a previous ward manager, and you are responsible for the quality and safety on your ward,\\\" Mrs Foskett said.\",\"attributes\":[]}}]}},{\"type\":\"paragraph\",\"model\":{\"text\":\"She told the hearing that a member of staff who was leaving raised concerns about Ms Povall's behaviour in November 2017, # Nurse can continue to work in current role - hearing

![The entrance to an NHS hospital, which has blue signage with white writing indicating it is The Robert Jones and Agnes Hunt Orthopaedic Hospital. There is a pavement bounded by grass verges and road markings at a junction as well as double yellow lines.](https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/ace/standard/972/cpsprodpb/d58b/live/5091c9e0-831c-11f0-848b-c1258325c24c.jpg)Image source, GoogleImage caption, 

Deborah Povall was the manager at Powys Ward at the Robert Jones and Agnes Hunt Orthopaedic Hospital

ByPaul RogersLocal Democracy Reporter

- Published29 August 2025

**A nurse who said she had to stop herself from "gouging a former colleague's eyeballs out and shoving them down her throat" has been allowed to continue working in her current role.**

Deborah Povall was the manager of Powys ward at the Robert Jones and Agnes Hunt (RJAH) Orthopaedic Hospital in Gobowen, Shropshire, at the time she sent the text message to another colleague in 2018.

She is currently facing a Nursing and Midwifery Council (NMC) hearing into allegations over how she treated staff and the care received by patients at the hospital.

The NMC has now closed its case, with Ms Povall due to give evidence when it resumes in December.

Ms Povall, who was suspended after an investigation was launched in 2019, has admitted working excessive hours but denied all of the other allegations relating to incidents between 2017 and 2019.

The hearing heard on Thursday that since leaving the hospital, Ms Povall has been working for a company that provides healthcare assessments for the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP).

The panel decided not to impose an interim order, meaning she can continue working in her current role.

Ms Povall told the hearing she only works her current hours in her role and does not use social media or text.

"My current role requires me to be a registered nurse; therefore, any interim order potentially may mean that I cannot do that depending on what that was," she explained.

"My role involves me talking to individuals that are not acutely unwell. However, they have healthcare conditions, and my job is to ascertain them and the impact on their day-to-day function."

Alastair Kennedy, case presenter for the NMC, confirmed Ms Povall has been free to work since the investigation began.

"Two charges have been proved by way of admission: working excessive hours and sending an unfortunate text message," he said.

"Neither of these issues raises public protection concerns to the extent that an interim order will be required. There is unlikely to be a repetition of working excessive hours or the text issue."

Witness Kirsty Foskett said she had a good working relationship with Ms Povall but did speak to her about working too many hours, as she "felt a need to cover vacant shifts".

Mrs Foskett, who was the NMC's final witness, was the surgical matron of the five wards at the hospital at the time.

The hearing heard Ms Povall would have the first level of approval for rotas, followed by herself.

"I had been a previous ward manager, and you are responsible for the quality and safety on your ward," Mrs Foskett said.

She told the hearing that a member of staff who was leaving raised concerns about Ms Povall's behaviour in November 2017, but she was "quite open" and "reflective" to the feedback.

The hearing is due to continue on 15 December.

*This news was gathered by the* [*Local Democracy Reporting Service*](/lnp/ldrs)*, which covers councils and other public service organisations.*

# Nurse can continue to work in current role - hearing

![The entrance to an NHS hospital, which has blue signage with white writing indicating it is The Robert Jones and Agnes Hunt Orthopaedic Hospital. There is a pavement bounded by grass verges and road markings at a junction as well as double yellow lines.](https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/ace/standard/972/cpsprodpb/d58b/live/5091c9e0-831c-11f0-848b-c1258325c24c.jpg)Image source, GoogleImage caption, 

Deborah Povall was the manager at Powys Ward at the Robert Jones and Agnes Hunt Orthopaedic Hospital

ByPaul RogersLocal Democracy Reporter

- Published29 August 2025

**A nurse who said she had to stop herself from "gouging a former colleague's eyeballs out and shoving them down her throat" has been allowed to continue working in her current role.**

Deborah Povall was the manager of Powys ward at the Robert Jones and Agnes Hunt (RJAH) Orthopaedic Hospital in Gobowen, Shropshire, at the time she sent the text message to another colleague in 2018.

She is currently facing a Nursing and Midwifery Council (NMC) hearing into allegations over how she treated staff and the care received by patients at the hospital.

The NMC has now closed its case, with Ms Povall due to give evidence when it resumes in December.

Ms Povall, who was suspended after an investigation was launched in 2019, has admitted working excessive hours but denied all of the other allegations relating to incidents between 2017 and 2019.

The hearing heard on Thursday that since leaving the hospital, Ms Povall has been working for a company that provides healthcare assessments for the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP).

The panel decided not to impose an interim order, meaning she can continue working in her current role.

Ms Povall told the hearing she only works her current hours in her role and does not use social media or text.

"My current role requires me to be a registered nurse; therefore, any interim order potentially may mean that I cannot do that depending on what that was," she explained.

"My role involves me talking to individuals that are not acutely unwell. However, they have healthcare conditions, and my job is to ascertain them and the impact on their day-to-day function."

Alastair Kennedy, case presenter for the NMC, confirmed Ms Povall has been free to work since the investigation began.

"Two charges have been proved by way of admission: working excessive hours and sending an unfortunate text message," he said.

"Neither of these issues raises public protection concerns to the extent that an interim order will be required. There is unlikely to be a repetition of working excessive hours or the text issue."

Witness Kirsty Foskett said she had a good working relationship with Ms Povall but did speak to her about working too many hours, as she "felt a need to cover vacant shifts".

Mrs Foskett, who was the NMC's final witness, was the surgical matron of the five wards at the hospital at the time.

The hearing heard Ms Povall would have the first level of approval for rotas, followed by herself.

"I had been a previous ward manager, and you are responsible for the quality and safety on your ward," Mrs Foskett said.

She told the hearing that a member of staff who was leaving raised concerns about Ms Povall's behaviour in November 2017, but she was "quite open" and "reflective" to the feedback.

The hearing is due to continue on 15 December.

*This news was gathered by the* [*Local Democracy Reporting Service*](/lnp/ldrs)*, which covers councils and other public service organisations.*
